segments.title: Why superfiring turrets needed the right hull form
segments.summary: A design question explains why early battleships needed sufficient freeboard and stability before they could use higher-mounted guns effectively.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke distinguishes the broad idea of guns firing over another mount from the later, familiar superfiring-turret arrangement. He argues that earlier ships' low freeboard and tight metacentric-height margins constrained where heavy mounts could go. Deeper-draft, higher-freeboard designs changed those limits, and he points to the Virginia class as evidence that the arrangement had become practicable. The answer also notes that rougher waters made freeboard a more pressing operational issue for some navies and routes.
segments.question: Why were more six-inch guns not mounted in superfiring positions on earlier warships, and would higher freeboard create excessive topweight?
segments.answerShort: The arrangement was technically possible, but low-freeboard ships had tighter stability constraints. Higher freeboard and deeper-draft hulls made elevated mounts more practical, while also improving usefulness in rough seas.

evidence.note: He relates superfiring mounts to freeboard, metacentric height, the Virginia class, and operating conditions.
